speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 5 of the interview with Alexander Riseman in Hendon, London, February 23, 1997.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --as I was telling you about my brother, so we decided my brother should be the one to be deported. He got up in the morning. He had-- he-- he-- everybody had a rucksack. Wherever we went, we had a rucksack and a sort of a-- it wasn't a plate. It was a container, a soup container. Nobody moved without that soup container, in case you-- you were offered some soup, you should be able to-- prepared to put it in. So-- and the-- I went along with him to that place, the assembly place, where he was going to be deported. And we kissed each other. There wasn't much time, because I had to go to work. I had to be at work at a certain time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- and he went inside. And I went to work. I was sitting in-- in-- in the factory where I was working. It was-- when I took him there, it was about 8 o'clock, before I started work-- half-past 7:00.

speaker: interviewee
text: At about lunchtime, I looked out of the window, and I saw people running backwards and forwards in the street. And they were kissing each other. And they were hugging. There was dancing. I found out the deportations stopped. But I wasn't sure whether it's not too late. I was frightened that my brother had been sent away, already. I didn't care. I wasn't supposed to run out of the factory, because you had to check-in, check-out. I didn't take any notice. I ran out from the factory. And I ran out, ran towards that-- it was a prison, where they held these people, the people that were deported. And I saw my brother. He came out. And we embraced each other. And you can imagine the-- the-- the joy that he was saved. He wasn't deported. And he came back. And after that, my-- my brother and my mother managed to get a job, because we could see, whoever's not working doesn't stand a chance. We were hoping, if you're working-- he kept saying, if you're working, nothing will happen to you. This was his favorite slogan. Work saves you from all the trouble. If you're working, nothing will happen to you. So they managed to get jobs. My mother started working in a-- in a place where they sorted clothes, old clothes. My brother got a job in a metal factory, where they made all different kinds of machine tools. He learned. They taught him how to work. The clothes that my mother worked was-- they were bringing in tons and tons of-- of clothes from people. We didn't know where these clothes was coming from. And my mother was not allowed to say anything about her job. That's another thing. If-- if there-- if somebody-- if a rumor went around, and the police found out, from where this rumor originated, these people were picked up, right away, and were deported. So if-- even if you knew something, what's going on, you had to keep it to yourself. You were not allowed to tell anybody. But one day, I remember my mother came home. And she said, she found a-- a-- a-- a note in-- in one of the clothes that they sorted. And it said that the-- a man wrote it. And he said, we are queuing up now to go to the gas chamber. And we want-- you know, I'm writing this note. People should know what's happening to us. And this note, somehow, came back with the clothes. Later on, they found other things and-- and some of the things were from the ghetto. Money-- ghetto money was found, identification. We all had identity-- identity cards in the ghetto, so some identity cards from the ghetto. Some of these-- these clothes was from people that were taken away from the ghetto. So my mother came home that evening. And she was terribly upset. But she wasn't allowed to tell anybody about this. And she told us, but we were not mentioning it. We didn't mention it to anybody else. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was I? Yeah. During this time, you were very religious-- religion, keeping kosher. Shabbat played a very important life-- part in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did How did it go in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the beginning, we-- we did not-- we did get-- they started sending in horse meat into the ghetto. We didn't have any-- any kosher meat anymore. And for about a year, I didn't touch it. I was-- I was starving hungry, but I didn't touch it. I think my-- my-- my sister did eat it, not right in the beginning but later on. But after about a year, [LAUGHS] we couldn't even get a hold of it. They-- they didn't-- they didn't give it to us anymore. We would have eaten it, but we didn't get it. So-- and also, and-- and-- and when I started working in this factory, in the tailoring factory, we had to work on the shabbat. We worked six days a week, from-- from about half-past 7:00 or 8:00 in the morning till 6:00 in the evening. And on Sundays, we also worked a half a day. This was voluntary. But we were offered a-- a bowl of soup. For working Sunday, we got a-- a bowl of soup, so everybody volunteered to work on Sunday, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't have much free time. And when we came home in the evenings, we had to queue up for the rations, in the co-ops, to get the rations. And it was-- sometimes it took hours. And we came home, knocked out, until we managed to get the ration.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must mention, around that time, in 1941, a lot of Jews were brought in, from all different countries in Europe, into the ghetto, from Germany, from Austria, from Luxembourg, from France. Thousands-- about 40,000, 50,000 Jews came into the ghetto. And they were told that they're coming to an autonomous Jewish region, and they will be offered jobs in their professions. And they will be-- they will give-- be given a good living accommodation. And they-- they told us that, when they left their counties, they were put on proper passenger trains. But as they came near the Polish border, they were transferred from these trains into cattle wagons. And when they arrived in the ghetto, they were-- because they were told that this is only temporary, and they will be transferred back, again, into-- into passenger trains. And when they arrived in the ghetto, they realized what's happened to them. And they-- in the beginning, they-- they were taken aback. They looked at us. And we were already like skeletons in rags. And they came, the women, with nice hairstyles and beautiful outfits, the men in nice overcoats, with loads of luggage, they let them bring with. And in the beginning, they couldn't take the food, what they were offered. And we used to stand, queuing up, as they were throwing away the food they were offered. We used to pick it up, because we were starving already and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: I've got to go to an engagement. Will you be long? Because [? they'll go ?] first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, let them go first, yes. OK. So a lot of these young people managed to find jobs. They took any job just to stay alive. But the ones that didn't have jobs, after a few months, were deported. They had to report to an assembly place. And they were deported. Well, now we know what happened to these people. But at the time, they didn't realize. Neither did we. The-- the-- and the older people, the weak people died in the ghetto within a few months. They couldn't take this harsh life. And also, they started bringing in Jews from local-- from small towns around Lodz, from different places. And they told terrible stories. These people, the people that came in were mainly young, young people that could work. The-- the older people and the children, they told us, were taken away, and they never saw them again. This went on right through 1941. They brought in people, and they deported people all the time. And we carried on working, all of us, who were already working. And then somehow we managed until the beginning of 1942. They gave-- the-- the-- Rumkowski gave a speech. And he said he has to-- he has to give the Germans all the people that are not working. And the children and old people-- over 60, I think it was-- are going to be deported and all the children. Well, we felt more or less safe. We were-- we were all-- all were-- it-- it was children up to 10, the order. And so we felt-- the four of us, I'm talking about-- felt fairly safe, because we were all working. And my mother was, at that time, about 40, I think. And-- no, she was younger, about 38. Anyway. And- and so we-- we were fairly safe, that it didn't affect us. So we carried on working. And it-- this carried on they-- they took people. They bought people for a few months. Finally, it was in May, Rumkowski gave another speech. He liked giving speeches. There was a-- a large square there, where he used to call together the people to listen to him. And he had a loudspeaker. And he used to speak. And he was a very good orator.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said the-- the-- there's a shortage of food. And the-- the Germans are saying they cannot afford-- they can't feed so many people. So some of the people will have to be taken to other-- other places, where there is more food available. We didn't believe him, but what choice did we have? The people that were deported were deported. They couldn't do anything about it. He gave a warning, whoever hides anybody in their place, not only if these people are caught will be deported, but the people that have hidden them will also be all deported. And also, every block had a-- a watchman-- a caretaker. And this caretaker had a list of all the people, of all the young people, how old they are. And another thing, Rumkowski had to deliver to the Germans, every week, a list of all the people in the ghetto, how old they are, how many ill?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was still a hospital then, at the-- at the time, opposite from where I worked. So whoever is ill and so that the Germans should know whom-- whom to deport. You couldn't-- you couldn't hide yourself for long. In any case, there was no food. You see, everybody had a ration card. And the first thing, what they did is, if somebody was on the list to be deported, they took away his ration card. So the person had no food. And even if somebody shared a bit of food, what they had, it didn't help, because the police were-- used to make raids in the middle of-- the Jewish police used to raid, in the middle of the night, and get these people out, if-- wherever they hid. They couldn't hide for long.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this went on for a few months until it was in July, 1942. I was working in the factory. And as I told you before, opposite was this hospital, was a large Jewish hospital. It-- it had a few hundred inmates.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, lorries drove up, and they rushed into-- the German Gestapo, SS, and with Jewish police, as well. They rushed in, into that hospital. And the windows were opened. And they started throwing out these people, from the beds, onto lorries that were standing downstairs, from the third or fourth floor. That building had about four floors. It was a big building. And they started throwing, like sacks of potatoes. I've never-- I will never forget this sight. In my life, I'll never forget it. And they were showing them onto these lorries. And people heard about it. People that had children or relatives in this hospital came running toward the hospital to try to get them out, before they're being thrown out onto these lorries. And the police were pushing them away. And the-- the Germans started shooting, first in the air, then they started shooting into the crowd. And till finally, this hospital was emptied of people. And we were told, that evening, not to report the next day to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were big announcements made in the street. People-- there's a curfew. People should stay at home until further notice. So we stayed in our flat. In the flat? In the-- the room, it was on the second floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were sitting and waiting. And we heard shots in the street, coming from the street. We were sitting like this for two days. And then, all of a sudden, we heard a noise in the yard, shooting. The shooting was coming nearer and nearer. And even then, we-- we felt secure, because we always-- we thought that didn't mean us. Because we were working. And they only wanted the young people, the children, and the old people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- we felt that-- you know, you always hope for the best. They didn't mean us. So when we heard this noise, they started shooting. With a loud-hailer, they announced, everybody should come down onto the yard. And whoever is found in the apartment and didn't come down to the yard, would be shot on sight. So we went down. We came down. There was the Jewish police, with the German SS. He had this revolver in his hand, I remember, one of the officers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he started picking out people, to the left, to the right. And there were lorries waiting to be-- the people that he takes-- picks out, to be taken away, they should get onto the lorries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he picked out my younger brother and my mother. And I was left with my sister. So my sister, she was of a very-- she was very close to my mother. She didn't want to be separated. So she went over to my mother. So I was left alone. I didn't want to be left alone. So I also went over. And they put us all, the four of us, onto the lorry. And they drove us out into the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where they're taking us. But we knew they're not taking us to-- [LAUGHS] to a-- to a good place. As we came into this-- yeah, I must say, I mentioned this in my book. As we were standing there, there was a-- a young woman, and she had a-- a little girl, of about four. And he, this SS man, he order the girl-- the child should be separated from the mother. And she was holding onto this child. And she wouldn't part with the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told her to turn around. And he said to her, I give you two minutes to leave this child alone, otherwise I will shoot both of you. And she-- she didn't believe him. So he said to her, she should turn around with the child, and took out his revolver, and he shot them both, with two bullets, one each. And they were both dead. They fell down, just like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was standing there. And you can imagine what we felt like after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were terrified. So we were all-- we were taken onto the lorries and were driven out into the street. As I was passing, in the street, we had to pass this fish market, a square, it was a big square. And we saw the SS sitting in their cars. And they were drinking and laughing away as we were passing. And we saw about 30 people, on gallows, hanging. And not a sound came from the people that were on the wagon. It was so quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you think they were taking you? What did you think you were-- was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew we were going to be deported. We didn't know exactly where they're taking us, but we knew that we were going to be deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did deportation mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: By then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you volunteered, you volunteered for deportation. What did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they told us they're taking us for work. But we realized they're not taking little children and old people for work. So we-- this came to us as a shock, because we didn't-- we didn't-- we and my-- the four us-- of us didn't expect this, because we were all working. So we thought we were safe. Anyway, we were taken into this-- we were passing this market, after we passed this scene we saw. And all the-- the streets were-- the SS were running around on their motorbikes, up and down, looking for people, where they was-- [SOB] they could get hold of. There weren't. Officially, there was nobody. There was a curfew. Nobody was allowed to walk in the street. We came to this place of assembly, which was-- before the war, it was a hospital, an isolation hospital. It had a very big wall, a high wall, surrounded with-- with barbed-wire and glass on top of it. It hard-- people shouldn't be able to escape from it. Because there were-- it was a sort of a lunatic asylum, there, or people that had infectious diseases. We were taken into this hospital. And the screams and the shouting that was going-- coming out. It had-- it had big-- a big area outside the hospital, with trees and bushes, gardens there. And there was-- in the center was a big building, where the actual hospital was. And we knew, once we get-- they get us into this building, we've had it. We will never be able to escape. Because we could see, from the other side of the building, there were lorries coming and taking away these people. And the way they were throwing these people onto the lorries, they were not taking them for work. We realized what it's for.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we said to each other, we must find a way how to escape from this place. So-- and we were not the only ones. A lot of people were in the-- in the compound outside the building. And they're running. The police, the Jewish police were chasing us, from one place to the other, to try to get us into the building.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was getting dark. And we, somehow, managed to hide ourselves in these bushes. And at night, there was-- it was quiet. They stopped. The action stopped as when it got dark. We managed to hide ourselves the whole night. But in the morning, the whole-- the traffic started again. They were bringing people and taking out people. Every time the gate opened, where the people were coming in, there were hundreds of people trying to push themselves. As the lorries were coming in, they were trying to, through the side, to run out. I noticed a Jewish policeman there. His wife used to work for us before the war. And he knew my mother. No, first, he got a hold of my sister. My sister was, at the time, about 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, I will save you from here. He managed to get her a white overall, with an armband, with a red cross on it. And he put her on a lorry that was transporting the ill people in-- it was bringing in ill people. And when they brought in the ill people, the same lorry went out. So he got her on such a lorry. And she was out. We didn't know what's going to happen to her. She was out.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was the biggest problem to get out from there. Somehow or other, he-- he-- this policeman managed to get her out. And I was left with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people helped each other to get over this wall. One lifted up the other. One-- three or four people to jump over the wall. My brother somehow got over the wall, and he was out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was left by myself. After running backwards and forwards, I also managed to get out, over the wall. As I got-- I remember, I-- I fell off this, on the other side of the wall. I fell down. And there was shooting, from all sides, coming, shots were coming from all sides. And I-- I don't know where I got the strength from, because I hadn't eaten all this time, since they took us away. And I was running. I just got up, and I was running. And shots were coming. And I ran. I ran. It was an open field. Till I finally came to a block of flats from the ghetto. And I got into a-- a-- a building. And all the time, in the ghetto, in order to avoid being-- walking through the main roads, we used to knock out, from one building into the other, a wall, so that you can run a long way without having to go into the main road. Because I knew the main road is full with the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I run, like this, from one building to the other, till I finally manage to get back to the place where we live. And I-- I came up to the room where we lived. And there was nobody there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quiet. And I just sat down. I didn't know what's going to happen to me. I just sat down there. And I sat for a few hours, till it was getting dark. And in the evening, my mother turned up, my sister turned up, and my brother turned up. They all came together. And we just sat there and didn't know what's happening to us. There wasn't-- we had no food. Whatever food we did have before was stolen by the people that were left behind. They went around the rooms, and they took whatever food they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had nothing to eat. And we were frightened to tell anybody that we're back, because we were not supposed to be back. We sat like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, we heard some people moving about. So somehow, we managed to get something to eat from the other people. I must say, people helped each other as much as-- if one could help each other, as much as possible, under such circumstances.
